title: CF 1527C - Sequence Pair Weight
content:
The problem statement can be found here.

Constraints

  • $1\le N \le 10^5$
  • $1\le a_i \le 10^9$
  • Sum of $n$ over all test cases does not exceed $10^5$

Approach

The brute force can be done by selecting two indices $L<R$ in $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ time and using hashmap to obtain the weight for subsegment $[L,R]$ in $\mathcal O(1)$ time (how?) but this will certainly give TLE. The key observation is to note that a pair $(i,j)$ where $a_i=a_j$ contributes $+1$ to every subarray containing both the indices $i$ and $j$. What is the number of such subarrays? For the left end of subarray, we can choose anything from $\{0,1,\dots,i\}$, and for the right end we can choose anything from $\{j,j+1,\dots,N-1\}$, so total number of such subarrays is $(i+1)(N-j)$. Since the pair contributes $+1$ for every subarray containing it, the contribution of the pair to the total answer is also $(i+1)(N-j)$. Thus the total answer is the sum of contribution of every such pair in the array, i.e. $$\mathtt{ans}=\sum_{\substack{i<j \\ a_i=a_j}} (i+1)(N-j)$$ The main problem is to calculate this sum in better time than $\mathcal O(N^2)$. To do this, let us fix $j$. Now the sum can be written as $$\mathtt{ans}=\sum_{j=0}^{j=N-1}\sum_{\substack{i<j \\ a_i=a_j}} (i+1)(N-j)=\sum_{j=0}^{j=N-1}(N-j)\left(\sum_{\substack{i<j \\ a_i=a_j}} (i+1)\right)$$ Note that the sum in brackets can be computed in a running manner in $\mathcal O(1)$ by maintaining a prefix sum of indices of the previous occurences of $a_j$ in a hashmap. Thus, the problem has been solved in $\mathcal O(N)$ time.
